sections.heading: What are machine-readable theatre programmes?
body: Today, theatre programmes are mostly only readable by humans – as PDFs, HTML pages, in each house's own layout. Search engines, platforms and archives have to re-enter every entry by hand.
Machine-readable means: the data is published in an open format (JSON, based on Schema.org and CIDOC). Other software can then import it without human effort – securely, unambiguously, instantly.

sections.heading: Establish technical and semantic standards
body: Together with theatres, the Academy for Theatre and Digitality Dortmund and research partners (Fraunhofer FIT, NFDI4Culture, acatech) we are developing a consistent data model for theatre programmes.
It translates the vocabulary of the theatre world into open standards without changing how anything looks on the website.

sections.heading: Build the data transfer interface
body: Based on this model, we are building an interface that lets theatres send their programmes automatically to the Cultural Data Space, to platforms, archives and display systems.
No more copy & paste. Connect once, stay in sync.

sections.heading: What becomes possible
body: Online event platforms, public displays, automated press releases, casting platforms, archives – wherever theatre programme data is needed, it will flow directly from the theatre's own system.

sections.heading: Where we are
body: Phase 2 (pilot interface): in preparation with beta theatres.
Phase 3 (rollout to broader group of houses): planned from 2027.

sections.heading: Who is involved
body: Steering group: Deutscher Bühnenverein, Academy for Theatre and Digitality Dortmund.
Advisory board: Fraunhofer FIT, acatech, NFDI4Culture, BKM (federal & Hamburg).
Pilot theatres are currently being onboarded – see participation.
